perturbation.original_text: (d) HAVING HAD AN OPPORTUNITY TO CONSULT WITH COUNSEL, THE PARTIES HEREBY KNOWINGLY, VOLUNTARILY AND TO THE FULL EST EXTENT PERMITTED BY LAW WAIVE ALL RIGHTS TO TRIAL BY JURY AND AGREE THAT ANY AND ALL MATTERS SHALL BE DEC IDED BY A JUDGE .
perturbation.changed_text: (d) THE PARTIES HEREBY AGREE THAT ANY AND ALL MATTERS SHALL BE DECIDED BY A JUDGE .
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase about consulting counsel and knowingly waiving rights weakens the waiver and may be challenged in court, especially if one party was not represented by counsel. This may not meet the 'knowing and voluntary' standard required for waiving constitutional rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Code of Civil Procedure, 735 ILCS 5/2-1105
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Code of Civil Procedure states that a jury waiver must be 'knowingly' made. By removing the clause that parties consulted counsel, the contract creates ambiguity about the 'knowing' aspect of the waiver, creating a legal contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Illinois General Assembly - Illinois Compiled Statutes Home Legislation & Laws Senate House My Legislation Site Map Bills & Resolutions Compiled Statutes Public Acts Legislative Reports IL Constitution Legislative Guide Legislative Glossary Search By Number (example: HB0001) Search Tips Search By Keyword Search Tips Advanced Search Illinois Compiled Statutes ILCS Listing Public Acts Search Guide Disclaimer Information maintained by the Legislative Reference Bureau Updating the database of the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) is an ongoing process. Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law. For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. 735 ILCS 5/2-1105 (735 ILCS 5/2-1105) (from Ch. 110, par. 2-1105) (Text of Section WITH the changes made by P.A. 98-1132, which has been held unconstitutional) Sec. 2-1105. Jury demand. (a) A plaintiff desirous of a trial by jury must file a demand therefor with the clerk at the time the action is commenced. A defendant desirous of a trial by jury must file a demand therefor not later than the filing of his or her answer. Otherwise, the party waives a jury. If an action is filed seeking equitable relief and the court thereafter determines that one or more of the parties is or are entitled to a trial by jury, the plaintiff, within 3 days from the entry of such order by the court, or the defendant, within 6 days from the entry of such order by the court, may file his or her demand for trial by jury with the clerk of the court. If the plaintiff files a jury demand and thereafter waives a jury, any defendant and, in the case of multiple defendants, if the defendant who filed a jury demand thereafter waives a jury, any other defendant shall be granted a jury trial upon demand therefor made promptly after being advised of the waiver and upon payment of the proper fees, if any, to the clerk. (b) All jury cases shall be tried by a jury of 6. If alternate jurors are requested, an additional fee established by the county shall be charged for each alternate juror requested. For all cases filed prior to the effective date of this amendatory Act of the 98th General Assembly, if a party has paid for a jury of 12, that party may demand a jury of 12 upon proof of payment. (Source: P.A. 98-1132, eff. 6-1-15 .) (Text of Section WITHOUT the changes made by P.A. 98-1132, which has been held unconstitutional) Sec. 2-1105. Jury demand. (a) A plaintiff desirous of a trial by jury must file a demand therefor with the clerk at the time the action is commenced. A defendant desirous of a trial by jury must file a demand therefor not later than the filing of his or her answer. Otherwise, the party waives a jur
perturbation.justification: The original text includes a waiver of the right to a jury trial, explicitly stating that the parties have had the opportunity to consult with counsel and are knowingly and voluntarily waiving their rights. The changed text removes the explicit mention of consulting with counsel and knowingly waiving rights. According to the Law Explanation, the Illinois Code of Civil Procedure requires that a jury waiver be 'knowingly' made. The perturbation explanation also highlights that removing the clause about consulting counsel introduces ambiguity regarding the 'knowing' aspect of the waiver. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on the timing of a jury demand rather than the conditions for its waiver, the removal of the 'opportunity to consult with counsel' language directly impacts whether the waiver can be considered 'knowing,' thus creating a legal contradiction, as the changed text weakens the certainty that the waiver was knowingly made.

perturbation.original_text: (b) IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY, IN CONTRACT, IN TORT OR OTHERWISE, FOR ANY INDIRECT, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL, PUNITIVE OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES OF ANY NATURE OR KIND RELATING TO OR IN CONNECTION WITH SUCH PARTY’S PERFORMANCE OR NON -PERFORMANCE OF ITS OBLIGATIONS UNDER THIS AGREEMENT .
perturbation.changed_text: (b) IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY, IN CONTRACT, IN TORT OR OTHERWISE, FOR ANY INDIRECT, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES OF ANY NATURE OR KIND RELATING TO OR IN CONNECTION WITH SUCH PARTY’S PERFORMANCE OR NON -PERFORMANCE OF ITS OBLIGATIONS UNDER THIS AGREEMENT .
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'punitive' from the damages waiver might be problematic under Illinois law if one party's breach involves willful and wanton misconduct. Illinois law sometimes allows punitive damages despite contractual limitations if the conduct reaches a certain egregious level. This creates a legal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois law regarding punitive damages, such as Loitz v. Remington Arms Co., 138 Ill.2d 404 (1990)
perturbation.law_citation: Loitz v. Remington Arms Co., 138 Ill.2d 404 (1990)
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statute, this case (and others like it in Illinois) illustrate that limitations on liability, especially regarding punitive damages, are not always enforceable, especially in cases of willful and wanton misconduct. Removing 'punitive' may not be enough to shield a party from such damages, and the contract's attempt to do so could be seen as misleading.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation indicates that removing 'punitive' from the damages waiver may create a problem under Illinois law because it could shield a party from punitive damages even in cases of willful and wanton misconduct. The law explanation supports this by stating that limitations on liability, especially regarding punitive damages, are not always enforceable, particularly in cases of willful and wanton misconduct. The original text included a waiver for punitive damages, whereas the changed text omits it. By removing the express waiver, the contract becomes ambiguous and potentially misleading, creating a legal contradiction regarding punitive damages, as described in the perturbation explanation and law explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Within 15 days of written request from Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall promptly return Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and all copies thereof to Disclosing Party or destroy Disclosing Party’s Conf idential Information, in- cluding all copies thereof, purge its computer systems of such Confidential Information and cer- tify such destruction in writing signed by a duly authorized representative .
perturbation.changed_text: Within 15 days of written request from Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall promptly return Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and all copies thereof to Disclosing Party or destroy Disclosing Party’s Conf idential Information, in- cluding all copies thereof, purge its computer systems of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to certify the destruction of confidential information creates a legal risk. Without certification, there's no verifiable proof that the Receiving Party actually destroyed the information, increasing the risk of future leaks or misuse. This contradicts common law principles surrounding the duty of care and reasonable measures to protect trade secrets and confidential information, as it removes a standard and verifiable safeguard.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Illinois Trade Secrets Act doesn't explicitly mandate certification of destruction, it requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Removing the certification requirement could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable measures, especially if a data breach occurs after the NDA is terminated, weakening the Disclosing Party's claim under the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 775 ILCS 20/ Defense Contract Employment Discrimination Act. Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. HUMAN RIGHTS (775 ILCS 20/) Defense Contract Employment Discrimination Act. 775 ILCS 20/1 (775 ILCS 20/1) (from Ch. 29, par. 24a) Sec. 1. In the construction of this act the public policy of the state of Illinois is hereby declared as follows: To facilitate the rearmament and defense program of the Federal government by the integration into the war defense industries of the state of Illinois all available types of labor, skilled, semi-skilled and common shall participate without discrimination as to race, color, creed, sex, religion, physical or mental disability unrelated to ability, or national origin whatsoever. (Source: P.A. 99-143, eff. 7-27-15.) 775 ILCS 20/1.1 (775 ILCS 20/1.1) (from Ch. 29, par. 24a.1) Sec. 1.1. Short title. This Act may be cited as the Defense Contract Employment Discrimination Act. (Source: P.A. 86-1324.) 775 ILCS 20/2 (775 ILCS 20/2) (from Ch. 29, par. 24b) Sec. 2. Every person, firm, association or corporation and the subcontractor, agent, or employee of the same to whom has been awarded a contract and to whom shall be awarded a contract by the United States government or any agency thereof and every person, firm, association or corporation which has been authorized or directed or is engaged in the training of persons for skilled or semi-skilled positions of labor for the United States government or any agency thereof and every subcontractor of any such person, firm, association, or corporation is designated in this act a war defense contractor. (Source: Laws 1941, vol. 1, p. 557.) 775 ILCS 20/3 (775 ILCS 20/3) (from Ch. 29, par. 24c) Sec. 3. It shall be unlawful for any war defense contractor, its officers or agents or employees to discriminate against any citizen of the state of Illinois because of race, color, creed, sex, religion, physical or mental disability unrelated to ability, or national origin in the hiring of employees and training for skilled or semi-skilled employment, and every such discrimination shall be deemed a violation of this act. (Source: P.A. 99-143, eff. 7-27-15.) 775 ILCS 20/4 (775 ILCS 20/4) (from
perturbation.justification: The original text requires certification of destruction of confidential information. The changed text removes this requirement. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this creates a risk because there's no verifiable proof of destruction, potentially violating duty of care principles. The Law Explanation, referencing the Illinois Trade Secrets Act, states that removing the certification requirement could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, especially if a data breach occurs. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is about employment discrimination and irrelevant, the core issue is the removal of a verifiable safeguard, which potentially weakens the protection of trade secrets under a 'reasonable measures' standard as explained in the law explanation.

perturbation.original_text: (c) This Agreement shall be governed by, and interpreted in accordance with, the laws of the State of Illinois without regard to pr inciples of conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) This Agreement shall be governed by, and interpreted in accordance with, the laws of the State of Nevada without regard to pr inciples of conflicts of law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Illinois to Nevada, when the company's principal place of business is in Illinois, could be seen as an attempt to circumvent Illinois' consumer protection laws if the agreement affects Illinois residents. This is especially relevant because the contract is between a Vendor and LiiON, LLC, which is located in Illinois. This presents a legal contradiction if Nevada law offers less protection to the Vendor than Illinois law would.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois choice of law principles and public policy concerns, as applied in Illinois courts
perturbation.law_citation: Belleville Toyota, Inc. v. Toyota Motor Sales, U.S.A., Inc., 199 Ill. 2d 325, 770 N.E.2d 177 (2002)
perturbation.law_explanation: While this case doesn't directly state a statute being violated, it highlights the Illinois Supreme Court's stance on choice-of-law provisions. Illinois courts will not enforce a choice-of-law provision if it violates Illinois public policy. If Nevada law significantly weakens protections available to the Vendor under Illinois law, an Illinois court might refuse to enforce the Nevada choice-of-law provision, creating a legal contradiction.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law from Illinois to Nevada. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this change may circumvent Illinois' consumer protection laws, especially since the contract is between a Vendor and LiiON, LLC, located in Illinois. The Law Explanation indicates that an Illinois court might refuse to enforce the Nevada choice-of-law provision if Nevada law weakens protections available to the Vendor under Illinois law, creating a legal contradiction. Since there are no law snippets to evaluate, the change results in removing legally mandated consumer protection.
